places.name: Lokál Dlouhááá
places.price: $$ — hearty Czech mains, cheap tank beer
places.reviewSummary: The flagship of the beloved Lokál chain serving classic Czech pub food and unfiltered tank Pilsner Urquell. A long, buzzy beer hall locals actually love.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers rave about the freshest tank beer in the city and authentic svíčková, though the cavernous room gets loud and packed at peak hours.
places.localTip: Order the beer 'na hladince' (with a thick foam head) like a local, and book ahead for dinner — it fills up fast.
places.story: This is the original Lokál, opened in 2009 by the Ambiente group to prove a humble Czech pub could be done brilliantly. At nearly 70 meters, the bar is one of the longest in the country, and the Pilsner travels straight from copper tanks to your glass in days, never weeks. Locals say it tastes the way beer did before pasteurization stole its soul.
places.highlight: Don't miss the fried cheese (smažený sýr) with tartare sauce.

places.name: La Degustation Bohême Bourgeoise
places.reviewSummary: A Michelin-starred tasting-menu restaurant reinventing 19th-century Czech bourgeois cooking. Open kitchen, long degustation, polished service.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests praise the inventive multi-course journey and chef interaction at the counter, though a few note it's a splurge and the pace runs long.
places.localTip: Sit at the chef's counter to watch the cooks plate every course inches from you.
places.story: La Degustation digs into a 19th-century Czech cookbook by Marie B. Svobodová and reimagines its dishes with modern technique. It earned its Michelin star years ago and has held it through changing chefs, a rare feat in a city that long ignored fine dining. The whole meal is a love letter to Bohemian grandmother cooking, dressed up for a special night.

places.name: Eska
places.reviewSummary: A converted factory in trendy Karlín turning out modern Czech cooking, house-fermented everything, and exceptional bread from its open bakery.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners love the airy industrial space and the bread program, with a few noting tables are tight and it gets busy at brunch.
places.localTip: Come for breakfast and grab a loaf from the bakery counter on your way out.
places.story: Eska sits in a renovated industrial hall in Karlín, a district that flooded catastrophically in 2002 and reinvented itself as Prague's coolest neighborhood. The kitchen leans into Czech ingredients reimagined with fermentation, smoke, and ash, and its potato baked in old bread has become a city signature. It earned a Michelin Bib Gourmand for proving great food needn't break the bank.
places.highlight: Don't miss the potato baked in old bread with sour cream.

places.name: U Modré Kachničky
places.reviewSummary: An old-world Malá Strana restaurant famous for roast duck and Bohemian game, set in candlelit frescoed rooms. Romantic and unapologetically traditional.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers adore the tender duck and intimate hand-painted dining rooms, though some find prices steep for the portions.
places.localTip: The name means 'The Blue Duckling' — order the signature roast duck with red cabbage and dumplings.
places.story: Tucked on a quiet lane below the castle, 'The Blue Duckling' has served Bohemian game cooking since the early 1990s, when private restaurants reappeared after communism. The cozy rooms are hand-painted with murals, and the kitchen still butchers and roasts whole ducks the slow way. It's the kind of place Praguers save for anniversaries.

places.name: Pho Vietnam Tuan & Lan
places.reviewSummary: A no-frills neighborhood spot serving some of Prague's most authentic pho, reflecting the city's large and beloved Vietnamese community.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals praise the deeply flavored broth and honest prices, though the room is plain and seating is cramped.
places.localTip: Add fresh chili and a squeeze of lime, and try a Vietnamese iced coffee to finish.
places.story: Prague's Vietnamese community dates to the communist era, when students and workers arrived under socialist exchange programs, and many stayed to build the food culture the city now treasures. Spots like this in Holešovice serve pho that rivals anything in the old SAPA market across town. A steaming bowl here on a cold Prague day is pure comfort.

places.name: Sister's Bistro (Chlebíčky)
places.reviewSummary: A modern take on the Czech chlebíček, the classic open-faced sandwich, with creative toppings done fast and fresh. Great quick lunch.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the inventive sandwiches and friendly counter service, though seating is limited so many take away.
places.localTip: Grab two or three different chlebíčky — they're small, so mixing flavors is the way to go.
places.story: The chlebíček is a Czech institution, a slice of baguette piled with potato salad, ham, egg, and a swirl of mayonnaise, sold at every old-school deli. Sister's, from the Ambiente group, gives the humble snack a stylish modern glow-up without losing the soul. It's proof that even Prague's most everyday food can be done with real care.

places.name: U Fleků
places.reviewSummary: A 500-year-old brewery and beer hall pouring its own dark lager, with rowdy halls, an accordion player, and a garden. Touristy but historic.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the unique house-brewed dark beer and lively atmosphere, but warn the roaming waiters push shots of Becherovka onto your tab.
places.localTip: Wave away the Becherovka shots unless you want them — they're added to the bill automatically if you accept.
places.story: U Fleků has brewed beer on this spot since 1499, making it one of the oldest brewpubs on earth. Its dark, malty Flekovský ležák has only one recipe and is served nowhere else in the world. Yes, it's a tourist circus now, but standing in a 16th-century hall with a black lager in hand is a genuine slice of Prague history.
places.highlight: Don't miss the house dark lager — it's brewed nowhere else.

places.name: Kantýna
places.reviewSummary: A grand butcher-shop-meets-canteen serving Czech beef cuts, tartare, and grilled meats in a former bank hall. You order by weight at the counter.
places.googleReviewSummary: Carnivores praise the top-quality beef and dramatic marble interior, though the order-at-counter system confuses first-timers and it gets loud.
places.localTip: Take a paper ticket at the meat counter, point at what you want, and they grill it to order — pay by weight at the end.
places.story: Kantýna occupies a former bank in a soaring socialist-era hall, repurposed by the Ambiente group into a butchery and grill that celebrates Czech cattle nose-to-tail. You shuffle along the counter cafeteria-style, choosing cuts and sides, then eat under chandeliers. The beef tartare, mixed at your table, is a city benchmark.

places.name: Café Savoy
places.reviewSummary: A restored First Republic café with a protected neo-Renaissance ceiling, famous for breakfast, pastries, and its own bakery. Elegant and bustling.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the stunning gilded ceiling and excellent breakfasts, though weekends require a reservation and service can feel rushed.
places.localTip: Reserve a morning table and order the Savoy breakfast with a fresh-baked French croissant from their bakery.
places.story: Café Savoy opened in 1893 and survived the 20th century to be lovingly restored, its gold-and-fresco ceiling now a protected monument. It recreates the café culture of the First Czechoslovak Republic, when intellectuals lingered for hours over coffee. The pastry counter near the door is a temptation no one resists.
places.highlight: Don't miss the in-house bakery's Věneček cream puff.

places.name: Café Louvre
places.reviewSummary: A grand 1902 café once frequented by Einstein and Kafka, with pastel halls, a billiard room, and proper Viennese-style coffee and cakes.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers cherish the historic atmosphere and hot chocolate, though the place is large and popular so there can be a wait at the door.
places.localTip: Head to the billiard room upstairs for a quieter table and a game on the antique tables.
places.story: Opened in 1902, Café Louvre hosted Albert Einstein during his Prague professorship and the young writers of Kafka's circle. The communists shut it in 1948 and gutted the interior; it was painstakingly restored and reopened in 1992. Sipping coffee under its pink ceilings, you're sitting where physics and literature once argued over the same tables.

places.name: EMA espresso bar
places.reviewSummary: A bright, minimalist espresso bar that helped launch Prague's specialty-coffee scene, pulling carefully sourced single-origin shots.
places.googleReviewSummary: Coffee lovers praise the expert baristas and clean flavors, though seating is limited and it's mainly a stand-and-sip spot.
places.localTip: Pair a flat white with their cardamom bun — they often sell out by midafternoon.
places.story: When EMA opened in 2012, third-wave coffee was barely a whisper in Prague, a city loyal to its grand cafés and bitter espresso. It quietly raised the bar, sourcing beans like wine and training a generation of baristas. Today the scene is everywhere, but EMA's sunlit corner is where many locals say it truly began.

places.name: Café Slavia
places.reviewSummary: A storied riverside café opposite the National Theatre with Art Deco interiors and a famous view of Prague Castle across the Vltava.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the castle-and-river views and faded glamour, though some find the food merely average and prices view-driven.
places.localTip: Ask for a window table on the river side at dusk for the castle lit up across the water.
places.story: Café Slavia opened in 1884 and became the haunt of poets, dissidents, and a young Václav Havel, who later became president. Its windows frame the National Theatre and the castle, and the painting 'The Absinthe Drinker' hangs in tribute to its bohemian past. For over a century, revolutions were plotted over coffee at these very tables.

places.name: Kavárna Co Hledá Jméno
places.reviewSummary: A relaxed Smíchov café — its name means 'the café that's looking for a name' — known for all-day brunch and excellent coffee.
places.googleReviewSummary: Regulars love the chill vibe and big brunch plates, though tables fill up on weekend mornings and the wait can stretch.
places.localTip: Go on a weekday morning to snag a window seat and avoid the brunch-rush queue.
places.story: The playful name — 'The Café That's Looking For A Name' — came from owners who simply never settled on one, and it stuck. It became a flagship of Prague's brunch culture, serving eggs and good coffee in a neighborhood away from the tourist crush. Locals adopted it as a living-room away from home.

places.name: Vnitroblock
places.reviewSummary: A converted factory courtyard housing a café, sneaker shop, dance studio, and shops. The industrial-chic heart of hip Holešovice.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the photogenic industrial space and creative buzz, though it gets crowded and the coffee, while good, plays second to the vibe.
places.localTip: Order at the café, then wander the surrounding studios and design shops with your latte in hand.
places.story: Vnitroblock — Czech for 'inner block' — took over a disused factory in Holešovice and turned it into a multi-purpose creative hangout. Beneath exposed concrete and old machinery, you'll find latte art beside a wall of rare sneakers and dancers rehearsing upstairs. It captures exactly how this gritty district reinvented itself into Prague's design quarter.

places.name: Prague Castle
places.reviewSummary: The largest ancient castle complex in the world, towering over the city with St. Vitus Cathedral, palaces, and the Golden Lane within its walls.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are awed by the scale and the cathedral, though many warn of long security lines and crowds — arrive early.
places.localTip: Enter from the upper gate near Pohořelec to walk downhill through the complex and skip the steepest climb.
places.story: Founded around 880 AD, Prague Castle has been the seat of Bohemian kings, Holy Roman emperors, and now Czech presidents — a span of over eleven centuries. Guinness lists it as the largest coherent castle complex in the world, sprawling across 70,000 square meters. When the lights blaze at night, the whole hill looks like a crown floating above the river.
places.highlight: Don't miss the soaring nave and stained glass of St. Vitus Cathedral inside.

places.name: Charles Bridge
places.reviewSummary: The iconic 14th-century stone bridge lined with baroque statues, linking the Old Town to Malá Strana over the Vltava. Prague's most beloved walk.
places.googleReviewSummary: Everyone loves the statues and river views, but reviewers stress it's shoulder-to-shoulder by day — go at dawn for the magic.
places.localTip: Touch the bronze plaque of St. John of Nepomuk for luck, then come back at 6am for the bridge nearly to yourself.
places.story: Emperor Charles IV laid the first stone in 1357, supposedly at 5:31am on the 9th of July — a palindrome of odd numbers chosen for cosmic strength: 1357-9-7-5-31. For 400 years it was the only bridge across the Vltava, and its 30 baroque saints have watched over crossings for centuries. Legend says egg yolks were mixed into the mortar to bind it.
places.highlight: Don't miss sunrise here, when the statues glow and the crowds vanish.

places.name: Prague Astronomical Clock (Orloj)
places.reviewSummary: A 15th-century medieval astronomical clock on the Old Town Hall, putting on an hourly show of moving apostles. The world's oldest still operating.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors find the clock face beautiful and the tower view superb, though many call the hourly apostle show underwhelming versus the hype.
places.localTip: Skip fighting for the hourly show and climb the Old Town Hall tower instead for the best rooftop panorama of the square.
places.story: First installed in 1410, the Orloj is the oldest astronomical clock still running in the world. Legend says the city blinded its maker, Hanuš, so he could never build another — and in revenge he reached into the mechanism and stopped it for years. Each hour, Death rings his bell and twelve apostles parade past the little windows above the crowd.

places.name: Old Town Square
places.reviewSummary: The historic heart of Prague, ringed by the Týn Church's twin spires, baroque facades, and the Astronomical Clock. The city's grand living room.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the fairy-tale architecture and atmosphere, though they note relentless crowds and overpriced terrace cafés.
places.localTip: Look down for the 27 white crosses in the pavement marking executed Protestant nobles from 1621.
places.story: This square has been Prague's beating heart for a thousand years, hosting coronations, markets, and executions alike. In 1621, 27 Czech leaders were beheaded here after the Battle of White Mountain, and crosses in the cobbles still mark the spot. Around it rise the Gothic Týn Church and the baroque mansions that survived every century of upheaval.

places.name: Wenceslas Square
places.reviewSummary: A grand boulevard-square topped by the National Museum and the statue of St. Wenceslas, where the Velvet Revolution unfolded in 1989.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers value its history and energy but caution it's more commercial strip than pretty square, with pickpockets at night.
places.localTip: Find the memorial to Jan Palach in the pavement below the museum, where he gave his life protesting the 1968 invasion.
places.story: More boulevard than square, this is where modern Czech history happened. In November 1989, hundreds of thousands jangled their keys here, calling for the end of communism in the peaceful Velvet Revolution. At its top, the mounted statue of St. Wenceslas, the patron saint, has watched over every uprising since the 19th century.

places.name: St. Nicholas Church (Malá Strana)
places.reviewSummary: The finest high-baroque church in Prague, with a vast frescoed dome and an organ once played by Mozart. A jaw-dropping interior.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are stunned by the ceiling fresco and ornate sculpture, though some note the entry fee and that it's a working concert venue.
places.localTip: Catch one of the evening classical concerts here to hear the Mozart-era organ fill the dome.
places.story: Built by the Dientzenhofer family of architects in the 1700s, St. Nicholas is the crown jewel of Bohemian baroque. Mozart himself played its grand organ in 1787, and his requiem was performed here after his death. The ceiling fresco of St. Nicholas is one of the largest in Europe, swirling with cherubs across nearly 1,500 square meters.

places.name: Petřín Lookout Tower
places.reviewSummary: A 63-meter steel lookout tower atop Petřín hill, often called Prague's little Eiffel Tower, reached by a charming funicular through orchards.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the panoramic climb and the funicular ride, though the spiral staircase is a workout and windy at the top.
places.localTip: Use a regular Prague transit ticket on the funicular up, then walk down through the blossoming gardens in spring.
places.story: Built for the 1891 Jubilee Exhibition and inspired by the Eiffel Tower it echoes in miniature, the Petřín tower stands on a hill that puts its tip higher than its Parisian cousin. The funicular that carries you up has run, with pauses, since the same year. From the top, the whole red-roofed city and its castle unfurl below.

places.name: Old Jewish Cemetery (Josefov)
places.reviewSummary: A hauntingly crowded medieval Jewish cemetery where some 12,000 tombstones tilt over layers of graves, in the historic Josefov quarter.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors find it deeply moving and atmospheric, though it's part of a paid museum circuit and gets busy with tour groups.
places.localTip: Buy the combined Jewish Museum ticket to also see the nearby Spanish Synagogue and Pinkas Synagogue's Holocaust memorial.
places.story: For over 300 years, from the 15th to 18th centuries, this was the only burial ground permitted to Prague's Jews, so the dead were laid in up to twelve layers, pushing older stones upward. The result is a forest of crooked tombstones, around 12,000 of them, leaning together like a crowd. The Renaissance scholar Rabbi Loew, linked to the Golem legend, rests here.

places.name: Riegrovy sady
places.reviewSummary: A sprawling Vinohrady park with a legendary beer garden and a grassy slope where locals gather to watch the sun set over the castle skyline.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals love the sunset views and laid-back beer garden, though the garden gets packed on warm evenings and service is slow.
places.localTip: Bring a blanket to the western slope around 8pm in summer for the city's best free sunset spot.
places.story: Riegrovy sady is where Vinohrady comes to breathe, a rolling green park named after a 19th-century Czech politician. On the grassy hill facing west, hundreds of locals gather at dusk with beers and guitars as the castle silhouette catches the last light. The big beer garden under the trees has poured cheap Pilsner for generations.
places.highlight: Don't miss the sunset from the western meadow.

places.name: Náplavka Farmers Market
places.reviewSummary: A Saturday farmers market strung along the riverbank embankment, with local produce, street food, and a buzzing waterfront crowd.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals love the riverside setting and food stalls, though it's busiest mid-morning and parking is impossible — come by tram.
places.localTip: Arrive before 10am on Saturday for the best pastries, then linger with a coffee on the river wall.
places.story: The náplavka — the riverside embankment — was once a working dock for barges, its vaulted cellars used for ice and storage. Today those arches hold cafés and bars, and every Saturday the quay fills with farmers selling cheese, cider, and fresh bread. It's the most local of Prague mornings, with the river sliding by below.

places.name: Letná Park & Beer Garden
places.reviewSummary: A clifftop park above the river with a famous beer garden, a giant metronome where Stalin's statue once stood, and sweeping city views.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the panoramic beer garden and relaxed crowd, though the kiosk is cash-friendly and seating fills fast at sunset.
places.localTip: Grab a plastic cup of beer from the garden kiosk and walk to the metronome for the best bridge-spanning view.
places.story: Letná crowns the cliffs above the Vltava, and where a giant statue of Stalin once loomed, a giant red metronome now ticks — a witty reminder that time moves on from tyrants. Skaters and dog-walkers fill the paths, and the beer garden under the chestnuts is a summer institution. The view down over the river's bridges is one Praguers never tire of.
places.highlight: Don't miss the giant metronome on the old Stalin monument plinth.

places.name: Náměstí Míru
places.reviewSummary: The elegant heart of residential Vinohrady, anchored by the neo-Gothic St. Ludmila church and ringed by cafés, theatre, and leafy streets.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals love the refined local atmosphere and the church, though there's little for tourists beyond a pleasant stroll and good coffee.
places.localTip: Come during the Christmas or Easter markets here — they're smaller and far more local than the Old Town ones.
places.story: Náměstí Míru, 'Peace Square,' is the dignified center of Vinohrady, a 19th-century bourgeois district of grand apartment buildings. Its twin-spired St. Ludmila church sits atop the deepest metro station in the EU, plunging over 50 meters down. This is where well-heeled Praguers actually live, shop, and meet for wine.

places.name: Střelecký Island (Střelecký ostrov)
places.reviewSummary: A tranquil tree-lined island in the middle of the Vltava under Legion Bridge, with river views, a café, and summer film screenings.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the peaceful escape and central-yet-hidden feel, though it's small and the riverside benches fill on sunny days.
places.localTip: Walk down the staircase from Legion Bridge to reach it — many tourists pass right over without realizing it's there.
places.story: Střelecký means 'shooters' island,' named for the marksmen's guild that practiced here for centuries. The first Czechoslovak May Day celebration took place on this very spit of land in 1890. Now it's a leafy refuge mid-river, where you can sit at water level with the castle rising behind the bridges and forget you're in a capital city.

places.name: Vyšehrad Fortress
places.reviewSummary: A hilltop fortress and park south of the center with the neo-Gothic Basilica, a cemetery of Czech legends, and quiet views few tourists reach.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors call it a peaceful, less-crowded alternative to the castle with great views, though it's a bit of a walk uphill.
places.localTip: Find the graves of composer Dvořák and the painter Mucha in the Slavín cemetery beside the basilica.
places.story: Vyšehrad is wrapped in Czech founding myth — legend says Princess Libuše stood on this rock and prophesied a great city 'whose glory will touch the stars.' The hilltop holds a 1,000-year-old rotunda and a national cemetery where Dvořák, Smetana, and Mucha rest. Locals come for the ramparts and the river bend below, blissfully free of crowds.
places.highlight: Don't miss the views from the southern ramparts over the Vltava.

places.name: Prague Boats Vltava Cruise
places.reviewSummary: Sightseeing boat cruises along the Vltava passing under Charles Bridge and below the castle, from short loops to dinner cruises.
places.googleReviewSummary: Passengers love the unique water-level views and relaxed pace, though some find the narration thin and dinner-cruise food average.
places.localTip: Pick a sunset departure and sit on the open top deck for the best photos as the castle lights up.
places.story: Seeing Prague from the Vltava reframes the whole city — bridges arch overhead, the castle climbs the far bank, and weirs churn beside you. The river has carried timber, ice, and emperors for a millennium, and Smetana immortalized it in his symphonic poem 'Vltava.' A slow drift downstream is the city's history rolling past in real time.

places.name: Beer Spa Bernard
places.reviewSummary: A quirky wellness experience where you soak in a tub of warm beer ingredients with unlimited Bernard lager on tap beside you.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests find it a fun, relaxing novelty with great beer, though it's pricey and clearly aimed at tourists having a laugh.
places.localTip: Book a couples tub and pace yourself on the self-pour tap — it flows the whole session.
places.story: Beer spas tap into a genuine Czech belief that hops and yeast are good for the skin, blending the country's two loves: bathing and beer. You lower into a wooden tub steeped in barley, hops, and brewer's yeast while pulling your own pints from a tap at the rim. It's gloriously silly and surprisingly soothing.

places.name: Ta Fantastika Black Light Theatre
places.reviewSummary: A uniquely Prague art form: actors in black against UV-lit sets create floating illusions in wordless, surreal performances.
places.googleReviewSummary: Audiences find the visual illusions inventive and family-friendly, though some say the loose plots can drag in the middle.
places.localTip: Sit in the center, several rows back, for the full depth effect of the floating-object illusions.
places.story: Black light theatre is a Prague specialty born from the city's avant-garde scene of the 1960s. Performers dressed entirely in black vanish against a dark stage, so glowing puppets and props seem to drift and dance on their own under ultraviolet light. It's pure visual poetry, needing no language to follow.

places.name: Historic Tram Line 23
places.address: Boards at Královský letohrádek stop, Praha 6
places.reviewSummary: A scenic route run partly by vintage 'T3' trams, winding past the castle, river, and old town on a normal transit fare.
places.googleReviewSummary: Riders love the nostalgic vintage cars and the cinematic route, though the heritage cars don't run on every departure.
places.localTip: Use a standard 90-minute ticket and ride the full scenic stretch from Malá Strana up to the castle district.
places.story: Prague's red trams have rattled through the streets since 1891, and Line 23 threads the most beautiful of them all, sometimes using restored mid-century T3 cars. There's no better budget tour: for the price of a transit ticket you glide past the castle, over the river, and through cobbled lanes. Locals just call it the best seat in the city.

places.name: Anonymous Bar
places.reviewSummary: A theatrical Guy Fawkes / 'Anonymous'-themed cocktail bar with smoke, masks, and an interactive flaming-drinks menu.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the immersive theatrics and skilled bartenders, though it's dim, snug, and reservations are essential on weekends.
places.localTip: Order from the 'Molecular' or interactive section of the menu to get the full smoke-and-fire show at your table.
places.story: Inspired by the hacktivist 'Anonymous' aesthetic and the film V for Vendetta, this bar leans hard into mystery, masks, and showmanship. Bartenders torch, smoke, and theatrically present cocktails that arrive in skulls and clouds of vapor. It regularly ranks among the world's best bars, proving Prague's cocktail scene has serious flair.

places.name: Museum of Communism
places.reviewSummary: An unflinching museum on daily life under Czechoslovak communism, from propaganda and shortages to the secret police and Velvet Revolution.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors find it informative and sobering, with a recreated interrogation room, though some wish for more interactive displays.
places.localTip: Allow at least 90 minutes — the final section on the 1989 revolution is the emotional payoff.
places.story: Czechoslovakia lived under one-party communist rule from 1948 until the Velvet Revolution of 1989, and this museum lays bare what those four decades felt like. You walk through a recreated grocery store of empty shelves, a classroom of propaganda, and a chilling interrogation room. It's a vital counterweight to the city's fairy-tale prettiness.

places.name: DOX Centre for Contemporary Art
places.reviewSummary: Prague's leading contemporary art center in a converted Holešovice factory, with bold exhibitions and the wooden airship 'Gulliver' on the roof.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors praise the thought-provoking shows and striking architecture, though some find the exhibitions challenging and the café small.
places.localTip: Climb up to the Gulliver airship, a 42-meter wooden zeppelin-shaped reading room on the roof terrace.
places.story: DOX opened in 2008 in a renovated factory, becoming the boldest contemporary art space in the country, unafraid of political themes. Its rooftop is crowned by 'Gulliver,' a giant wooden airship you can walk inside, used for readings and talks. It anchors Holešovice's transformation from industrial backwater to creative district.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Gulliver airship on the roof.

places.name: Mucha Museum
places.reviewSummary: A museum devoted to Alphonse Mucha, the Czech master of Art Nouveau, with his famous Paris theatre posters and decorative panels.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors adore the iconic flowing posters and the short film, though several note it's a compact museum for the ticket price.
places.localTip: To see Mucha's monumental work, pair this with his stained-glass window inside St. Vitus Cathedral at the castle.
places.story: Alphonse Mucha became the defining artist of Art Nouveau almost by accident, when he designed a last-minute poster for actress Sarah Bernhardt in 1894 Paris and woke up famous. His swirling goddesses wrapped in flowers became the look of an era. Late in life he returned to his homeland and poured his fortune into his patriotic 'Slav Epic' canvases.

places.name: Veletržní Palác (National Gallery)
places.reviewSummary: The National Gallery's modern and contemporary collection in a landmark functionalist palace, including Mucha's monumental Slav Epic cycle.
places.googleReviewSummary: Art lovers praise the vast collection and the building itself, though the scale is exhausting and signage can be sparse.
places.localTip: Go straight for the Slav Epic hall if time is short — the 20 giant canvases are overwhelming in person.
places.story: The Veletržní Palác, finished in 1928, was one of the world's first functionalist buildings, so radical that Le Corbusier praised and envied it. It now houses Czech and European modern art, and since recently displays Mucha's epic 20-canvas history of the Slavic peoples. Standing before those room-filling paintings is a humbling experience.
places.highlight: Don't miss the monumental Slav Epic canvases.

places.name: Kunsthalle Praha
places.reviewSummary: A sleek private art kunsthalle in a converted 1930s electrical substation near the castle, hosting major rotating modern-art shows.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the bold exhibitions and rooftop café views, though it's exhibition-only with no permanent collection so check what's on.
places.localTip: Head up to the rooftop café-bar for a drink with a close-up castle view, even without a ticket.
places.story: Kunsthalle Praha opened in 2022 inside the old Zenger substation, which once powered the city's trams. A family foundation transformed it into a gleaming private art hall, signaling Prague's growing confidence in contemporary culture. Its rooftop terrace, almost level with the castle hill, is a reward in itself.

places.name: Franz Kafka Rotating Head (by David Černý)
places.reviewSummary: A mesmerizing 11-meter mirrored sculpture of Kafka's head, made of 42 rotating layers that endlessly assemble and dissolve his face.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are captivated by the hypnotic motion and reflections, though it's tucked in a mall courtyard that's easy to miss.
places.localTip: Wait for a full rotation cycle — the head fully forms, then splits apart, every few minutes.
places.story: Sculptor David Černý, Prague's most provocative artist, built this kinetic monument to Franz Kafka from 42 independently spinning stainless-steel slices. As they turn, the writer's face materializes and shatters again, a fitting tribute to a man tormented by identity and bureaucracy. Kafka was born and lived almost his whole life in this very city.
places.highlight: Don't miss a full rotation as the face forms and breaks apart.

places.name: Municipal House (Obecní dům)
places.price: $$ — guided tours, café free to enter
places.reviewSummary: Prague's Art Nouveau masterpiece, a 1912 civic palace dripping with mosaics and Mucha murals, home to Smetana Hall and a glorious café.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are dazzled by the opulent interiors and the café, though the finest rooms require a guided tour ticket.
places.localTip: Even without a tour, step into the ground-floor café to admire the chandeliers and stained glass for the price of a coffee.
places.story: The Municipal House, completed in 1912, is the high temple of Czech Art Nouveau, decorated by the era's greatest artists including Mucha, who painted its mayoral hall. In 1918, Czechoslovak independence was proclaimed from its balcony. Inside, Smetana Hall hosts the opening concert of the Prague Spring festival every May beneath gilded ceilings.

places.name: Museum of Decorative Arts (UPM)
places.reviewSummary: A richly restored 19th-century museum of Czech glass, furniture, fashion, and design, showcasing Bohemia's world-famous craft traditions.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the glass collection and the renovated building, though it's quieter and less known than the city's bigger museums.
places.localTip: Focus on the Bohemian glass and garnet rooms — these are the crafts the region is genuinely world-famous for.
places.story: Bohemia has been a glassmaking powerhouse for 700 years, and this museum, founded in 1885, is its showcase. Crystal, garnets, posters, and Art Deco furniture trace how Czech design shaped European taste. The building, recently renovated, looks across to the Old Jewish Cemetery, layering centuries of the city's story side by side.

places.name: National Theatre (Národní divadlo)
places.reviewSummary: The golden-roofed neo-Renaissance home of Czech opera, ballet, and drama on the riverbank, a symbol of the nation's cultural rebirth.
places.googleReviewSummary: Audiences love the lavish gilded interior and world-class productions, though legroom is tight in the historic upper tiers.
places.localTip: Affordable last-minute and standing tickets are often released — check the box office on the day.
places.story: The National Theatre was funded by ordinary Czechs' donations as a statement of national pride, opening in 1881 — then burning down weeks later. The people raised the money again in just six weeks, and it reopened in 1883 under the motto 'The Nation to Itself,' carved above the stage. It remains the beating heart of Czech performing arts.

places.name: Rudolfinum (Dvořák Hall)
places.reviewSummary: A magnificent neo-Renaissance concert hall on the river, home of the Czech Philharmonic and famed for superb acoustics in Dvořák Hall.
places.googleReviewSummary: Concertgoers rave about the acoustics and grand interior, though tickets for top orchestras sell out far in advance.
places.localTip: Book a Czech Philharmonic concert in Dvořák Hall — the acoustics are considered among Europe's finest.
places.story: Opened in 1885 and named for Crown Prince Rudolf, the Rudolfinum is where Antonín Dvořák himself conducted the Czech Philharmonic's very first concert in 1896. The Nazis once ordered a statue of the Jewish composer Mendelssohn removed from its roof, leading to a famously bungled search. Today its Dvořák Hall is a temple of Czech classical music.

places.name: Jazz Dock
places.reviewSummary: A modern riverfront jazz club built right on the water, with nightly live sets and floor-to-ceiling windows over the Vltava.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the intimate setting and quality musicians, though tables near the stage are small and fill quickly — arrive early.
places.localTip: Reserve a riverside table and come for the earlier set, which tends to feature the headline acts.
places.story: Jazz Dock floats at the river's edge in Smíchov, its glass walls reflecting the Vltava as music plays. Opened in 2009, it became one of the city's most respected stages, hosting Czech and international acts almost every night. Prague's jazz roots run deep — the music thrived even as a subtle form of freedom under communism.

places.name: Reduta Jazz Club
places.reviewSummary: The oldest jazz club in Prague, a dimly lit cellar where President Clinton once played saxophone with the band in 1994.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the historic atmosphere and live sets, though the room is small, cash-friendly, and seats are snug.
places.localTip: Look for the photo and plaque marking where Bill Clinton jammed on the sax with Václav Havel watching.
places.story: Founded in 1958, Reduta is the oldest jazz club in Prague and one of the oldest in Central Europe, a smoky cellar that kept the music alive through communist years. In 1994, US President Bill Clinton picked up a saxophone here and played for President Havel, a saxophone Havel had just gifted him. Jazz, here, was always quietly political.
places.highlight: Don't miss the spot where Clinton played sax for Havel.

places.name: Lucerna Music Bar
places.reviewSummary: A beloved concert hall in the historic Lucerna palace, famous for live gigs and its legendary, packed 80s/90s video party nights.
places.googleReviewSummary: Crowds love the nostalgic dance nights and the venue's character, though it gets sweaty and jammed at the famous retro parties.
places.localTip: Come Friday or Saturday for the 'Videoparty' 80s/90s night — it's a Prague institution that sells out.
places.story: The Lucerna palace was built in the early 1900s by the grandfather of Václav Havel, and its music bar has hosted everyone from local legends to touring acts. While there, find David Černý's cheeky sculpture of St. Wenceslas riding an upside-down dead horse, hanging in the arcade above. The weekend retro video parties are the stuff of local legend.
places.highlight: Don't miss Černý's upside-down horse statue in the passage.

places.name: Cross Club
places.reviewSummary: A mind-bending multi-floor club built from welded scrap metal and moving machine parts, with electronic music, a café, and a cinema.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are stunned by the steampunk interior and alternative crowd, though the layout is a maze and it's grungy by design.
places.localTip: Arrive while it's still light to fully appreciate the kinetic scrap-metal sculptures before the dancefloor fills.
places.story: Cross Club is unlike any venue on earth — every surface is built from salvaged industrial junk, gears, pipes, and cables, much of it whirring and lit up. Spread across floors of a building by Holešovice station, it's part nightclub, part café, part art installation. It grew organically over years, welded together by its founders into a living machine.
places.highlight: Don't miss the moving kinetic sculptures throughout the space.

places.name: Karlovy Lázně
places.reviewSummary: A famous five-floor club by Charles Bridge, each level a different genre and era of music, billed as Central Europe's largest club.
places.googleReviewSummary: Partygoers love the sheer variety across five floors, though many call it touristy, pricey at the bar, and watch your drinks.
places.localTip: Each floor is a different decade or genre — wander up and down to find your sound rather than committing to one.
places.story: Karlovy Lázně sits in a former public bathhouse right at the foot of Charles Bridge, its name literally meaning 'Charles Baths.' Five floors stack house, hip-hop, oldies, and dance pop, one of the largest clubs in Central Europe. It's a rite of passage for young visitors, loud, packed, and unforgettable for better or worse.

places.name: Hemingway Bar
places.reviewSummary: An intimate, Hemingway-themed cocktail bar with the largest absinthe and rum selection in the city and world-class drinks.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests rave about the expert cocktails and moody Prohibition-era vibe, though it's tiny so booking ahead is essential.
places.localTip: Reserve in advance and ask the bartender for an off-menu drink built to your taste — it's their specialty.
places.story: Named for Ernest Hemingway, who adored absinthe and rum, this snug bar keeps the Czech Republic's largest collection of both. Dark wood, leather, and old typewriters conjure a Prohibition speakeasy. It regularly lands on the world's best bars lists, helping put Prague on the global cocktail map alongside the city's famous beer.

places.name: Ankali
places.reviewSummary: A serious underground techno club with a top-tier sound system and international DJ bookings, the heart of Prague's club-music scene.
places.googleReviewSummary: Ravers praise the booking, sound, and no-phone dancefloor focus, though it's strictly late-night and not for casual visitors.
places.localTip: Things don't get going until well after midnight — pace your night and expect to stay until dawn.
places.story: Ankali emerged as Prague's answer to Berlin's club culture, a dark warehouse-style space devoted to electronic music done right. Its booking pulls serious international names, and a no-nonsense focus on the music and the sound system has earned it cult status. This is where the city's true club heads spend their weekends.

places.name: T-Anker Rooftop Bar
places.reviewSummary: A rooftop bar atop the Kotva department store with sweeping views over the Old Town spires and a strong Czech beer list.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the panoramic terrace and craft beer selection, though it gets crowded at sunset and there can be a wait for the lift.
places.localTip: Take the Kotva mall escalators to the top floor, then the final stairs to the roof — easy to miss from the street.
places.story: Perched on the roof of the brutalist Kotva department store, T-Anker offers one of the few open-air panoramas in the city center. From its terrace, the Gothic and baroque spires of the Old Town spread out below you. It pairs that view with a rotating menu of small Czech craft breweries, a newer wave beyond the big lagers.
places.highlight: Don't miss the sunset view over the Týn Church spires.

places.name: BeerGeek Bar
places.reviewSummary: A craft-beer mecca with around 30 rotating taps of Czech and international brews, drawing serious beer hunters to Vinohrady.
places.googleReviewSummary: Beer fans love the huge rotating tap list and knowledgeable staff, though the basement room is snug and can get loud.
places.localTip: Ask the staff for a taster flight to navigate the 30 taps — they're genuinely passionate and happy to guide you.
places.story: While Prague runs on classic lager, a craft revolution has bubbled up, and BeerGeek leads it with one of the country's best rotating tap lists. It pours everything from hazy Czech IPAs to wild international sours, a world away from the standard pilsner. For drinkers wanting to taste where Czech beer is heading, this is the spot.

places.name: Vzorkovna (Dog Bar)
places.reviewSummary: A sprawling underground labyrinth bar with cheap beer, eclectic junk decor, table football, and a resident dog — a студent favorite.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the surreal cavernous vibe and rock-bottom prices, though it's grungy, smoky, and the deposit-card system confuses newcomers.
places.localTip: You're given a chip card on entry that you load with cash and pay back at the end — don't lose it or you forfeit a deposit.
places.story: Hidden down an unmarked staircase off Národní, Vzorkovna — nicknamed the 'Dog Bar' for its famous resident hound — is a maze of cellars crammed with salvaged junk, swings, and dim corners. Students and night owls pack its tunnels for some of the cheapest beer in the center. It feels like stumbling into someone's chaotic underground dream.

places.name: Vinograf Senovážné
places.reviewSummary: A relaxed, knowledgeable wine bar showcasing Moravian and small-producer Czech wines, with good charcuterie and friendly sommeliers.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the focus on local Moravian wines and helpful staff, though the by-the-glass list rotates so favorites may sell out.
places.localTip: Ask specifically for a Moravian orange or pét-nat wine — the region's natural wines are a delicious surprise.
places.story: Beer may be the Czech religion, but the southern region of Moravia quietly makes excellent wine, and Vinograf is the place to discover it. Its sommeliers pour small-grower bottles you'll rarely see abroad, from crisp Veltliners to funky natural wines. It's the sophisticated, low-key counterpoint to Prague's roaring beer halls.

places.name: Glassblowing Workshop at Ajeto Glass
places.reviewSummary: A hands-on glassblowing experience where you shape molten Bohemian glass at the furnace under a master's guidance.
places.googleReviewSummary: Participants love making their own piece and the skilled instructors, though sessions are short and must be booked ahead.
places.localTip: Book in advance and wear closed shoes and cotton — you'll be working close to a 1,000°C furnace.
places.story: Bohemia has blown glass for over 700 years, its crystal prized in courts across Europe. In a hands-on workshop you grip the blowpipe yourself, gathering glowing molten glass and shaping it as artisans have for centuries. There's nothing like feeling that living, liquid material take form in your own hands.

places.name: Czech Cooking Class at Chefparade
places.reviewSummary: A hands-on cooking class where you make Czech classics like svíčková, goulash, and dumplings, then feast on your creations.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the friendly chefs and learning authentic recipes, though the morning market-tour versions run long.
places.localTip: Choose a class that includes making bread dumplings (knedlíky) from scratch — the trickiest and most rewarding Czech skill.
places.story: Czech home cooking centers on slow-braised meats and fluffy bread dumplings, dishes that take know-how to nail. In a cooking class, you'll master svíčková — beef in a creamy root-vegetable sauce that is the unofficial national dish. You leave understanding why every Czech grandmother guards her dumpling recipe.

places.name: Marionette Carving Workshop (Truhlář Marionety)
places.reviewSummary: A workshop and shop devoted to the Czech art of marionettes, where you can learn to assemble and paint your own wooden puppet.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the craftsmanship and making a keepsake puppet, though the hands-on sessions are intimate and limited in number.
places.localTip: Even if you skip the workshop, browse the shop — these are handmade artisan puppets, a world apart from the souvenir-stall versions.
places.story: Czech marionette puppetry is so cherished that UNESCO recognizes it as cultural heritage. During centuries of Austrian rule, traveling puppeteers kept the Czech language alive in their shows when it was suppressed elsewhere. Carving and stringing your own marionette connects you to that quietly defiant, beloved tradition.

places.name: Letterpress & Print Workshop at Frrr Studio
places.reviewSummary: A small Holešovice studio offering hands-on letterpress and screen-printing sessions on vintage machines, making your own prints to keep.
places.googleReviewSummary: Participants love the analog craft and patient instructors, though classes are tiny and book out quickly.
places.localTip: Sessions are small-group — message ahead to confirm a slot and the technique you want to try.
places.story: In the heart of creative Holešovice, a handful of studios keep analog printmaking alive on rescued vintage presses. Pulling a sharp inked print by hand from a heavy iron machine is deeply satisfying in a digital age. It's a chance to slow down and make something tactile in the city's design quarter.

places.name: Charles Bridge Tower Viewpoint
places.reviewSummary: The Old Town Bridge Tower offers a postcard view straight down the length of Charles Bridge toward the castle — the classic Prague shot.
places.googleReviewSummary: Photographers love the framed view down the bridge, though the staircase is steep and the top balcony is narrow.
places.localTip: Climb at first light to shoot the empty bridge leading the eye to the castle, with no crowds blocking the frame.
places.story: Built in the 14th century as a triumphal gate to the bridge, this Gothic tower is considered one of the finest in Europe. From its gallery you look straight down the cobbled span of Charles Bridge to the castle beyond — the single most photographed sightline in Prague. The carved kings and saints on its facade have greeted travelers for 650 years.
places.highlight: Don't miss the dawn shot down the empty bridge.

places.name: Letná Beer Garden Overlook
places.reviewSummary: A clifftop terrace in Letná Park with a sweeping view across the Vltava and its chain of bridges — a favorite at golden hour.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the wide bridge-spanning panorama and relaxed beer-garden setting, though the best railings get busy at sunset.
places.localTip: Shoot from the railing near the beer garden in late afternoon when the sun lights the river and the bridges line up.
places.story: From the edge of Letná Park, the Vltava curves below you, crossed by a procession of bridges that step across the city like stones. It's where photographers come for the wide view that postcards of the bridges can't quite capture. With a beer in hand and the sun going down over the spires, it's hard to leave.

places.name: Golden Lane (Zlatá ulička)
places.reviewSummary: A tiny lane of brightly colored miniature houses inside the castle walls, once home to castle staff and, briefly, Franz Kafka.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors find the toy-like cottages charming and photogenic, though it's cramped, ticketed, and jammed midday — go early or late.
places.localTip: Visit after 5pm in summer when the lane is free to enter and far emptier for photos.
places.story: Golden Lane is a row of doll-sized houses tucked against the castle's northern wall, built in the 1500s for the castle's marksmen and later home to goldsmiths, hence the name. Franz Kafka stayed at number 22 in 1916, writing in his sister's tiny blue cottage. The candy-colored facades make it one of the most photogenic corners in the whole castle.
places.highlight: Don't miss Kafka's tiny blue house at number 22.

places.name: Dancing House (Tančící dům)
places.reviewSummary: A landmark deconstructivist building shaped like a dancing couple, designed by Frank Gehry, standing in bold contrast to the old riverfront.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the playful curving architecture and rooftop view, though it's primarily an office building so interior access is limited.
places.localTip: Shoot it from across the river or the next bridge upstream to catch the full 'dancing' silhouette against the sky.
places.story: Completed in 1996 by Frank Gehry and Vlado Milunić, the Dancing House twists two towers into the shape of a couple mid-dance, nicknamed 'Fred and Ginger.' It rose on a plot left empty since American bombs hit it in 1945. Controversial at first for clashing with the baroque riverfront, it's now one of the city's most-loved modern icons.

places.name: Strahov Monastery Library
places.reviewSummary: Two breathtaking baroque library halls at Strahov Monastery, with frescoed ceilings and ancient globes — among the most beautiful libraries on earth.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are awed by the ornate Theological and Philosophical halls, though you view them from the doorways and can't walk inside.
places.localTip: Pay the small photo-permit fee at the desk if you want to take pictures inside the halls.
places.story: Strahov Monastery, founded in 1143, holds two of the most beautiful library halls in existence — the Theological and Philosophical Halls, their ceilings exploding with baroque frescoes. Among 200,000 volumes are texts that survived centuries of war and dissolution. Standing in the doorway, gilded and frescoed, it feels like stepping inside an illuminated manuscript.
places.highlight: Don't miss the panoramic view over the city from the monastery terrace.

places.name: Klementinum Astronomical Tower
places.reviewSummary: A baroque astronomical tower in the historic Klementinum complex, offering a 360° view over the Old Town's red rooftops and spires.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the rooftop panorama and the stunning Baroque library glimpse, though access is by timed guided tour only.
places.localTip: Book a timed tour slot in advance online — the tower climb only runs with the guided group and sells out.
places.story: The Klementinum is a vast former Jesuit college, home to a baroque library hall and an astronomical tower where, until 1918, a flag and cannon signaled noon to the city. Climb its narrow stairs for a 360-degree sweep over the Old Town's terracotta roofs and a hundred spires. It also houses one of the world's longest continuous weather records, kept since 1775.
